examples.title: Peterson's Algorithm for Two Processes
examples.description: Software solution for critical section problem for two processes using shared variables
examples.code:
SHARED VARIABLES:
  flag[2] : BOOLEAN  // initially FALSE
  turn : INTEGER     // initially 0

PROCESS 0:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    flag[0] := TRUE
    turn := 1
    WHILE (flag[1] AND turn = 1) DO
      // busy wait - remain in entry section
    END WHILE
    
    // CRITICAL SECTION
    critical_section()
    
    flag[0] := FALSE
    remainder_section()
  END WHILE
END

PROCESS 1:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    flag[1] := TRUE
    turn := 0
    WHILE (flag[0] AND turn = 0) DO
      // busy wait
    END WHILE
    
    // CRITICAL SECTION
    critical_section()
    
    flag[1] := FALSE
    remainder_section()
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Peterson's algorithm uses two mechanisms: 'flag' indicates desire to enter critical section, 'turn' decides who enters when both want to. The while loop implements busy waiting in the entry section. When both flags are true, the process whose turn is not specified must wait.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for entry and exit sections in terms of iterations (bounded)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only uses two shared variables regardless of process count

examples.title: TestAndSet Based Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Hardware-based mutual exclusion using atomic TestAndSet instruction
examples.code:
SHARED VARIABLE:
  lock : BOOLEAN  // initially FALSE

FUNCTION TestAndSet(var lock : BOOLEAN) : BOOLEAN
BEGIN
  // Hardware atomic instruction
  temp := lock
  lock := TRUE
  RETURN temp
END

PROCESS i:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    // Entry section - busy wait using TestAndSet
    WHILE TestAndSet(lock) = TRUE DO
      // lock is TRUE, wait
    END WHILE
    
    // CRITICAL SECTION
    critical_section()
    
    // Exit section
    lock := FALSE
    
    remainder_section()
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: TestAndSet atomically reads the old lock value and sets it to TRUE in one instruction. The entry section loops until TestAndSet returns FALSE (lock was FALSE). This ensures mutual exclusion but does not guarantee bounded waiting by itself. The exit section simply resets lock to FALSE.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per entry/exit attempt in atomic operations

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation of Critical Section
examples.description: Using binary semaphore (mutex) for mutual exclusion in critical section
examples.code:
SEMAPHORE mutex : INTEGER := 1

// wait() operation (atomic)
PROCEDURE wait(S : SEMAPHORE)
BEGIN
  S := S - 1
  IF S < 0 THEN
    block_this_process()
    // add to semaphore queue
  END IF
END

// signal() operation (atomic)
PROCEDURE signal(S : SEMAPHORE)
BEGIN
  S := S + 1
  IF S <= 0 THEN
    wake_up_one_process()
    // remove from queue
  END IF
END

PROCESS:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    wait(mutex)    // Entry section - acquire lock
    
    critical_section()  // Only one process here
    
    signal(mutex)  // Exit section - release lock
    
    remainder_section()
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: The binary semaphore initialized to 1 acts as a lock. wait() decrements to 0 and allows entry, subsequent wait() blocks. signal() increments and wakes blocked process if any. Unlike spinlocks, blocked processes do not consume CPU. This is a blocking lock implementation.
examples.timeComplexity: wait() and signal() are O(1) in atomic time, but waiting process is blocked (no CPU usage)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for semaphore plus O(n) for process queue when blocked

examples.title: Producer-Consumer Problem with Semaphores
examples.description: Classic producer-consumer synchronization problem solved using semaphores
examples.code:
CONSTANTS:
  BUFFER_SIZE : INTEGER := n

SHARED VARIABLES:
  buffer[0..n-1] : ARRAY
  in, out : INTEGER := 0
  
SEMAPHORES:
  mutex : INTEGER := 1     // Binary - mutual exclusion
  empty : INTEGER := n     // Counting - empty slots
  full : INTEGER := 0      // Counting - filled slots

PRODUCER:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    item := produce_item()
    
    wait(empty)     // Wait for empty slot
    wait(mutex)     // Enter critical section
    
    buffer[in] := item
    in := (in + 1) MOD BUFFER_SIZE
    
    signal(mutex)  // Exit critical section
    signal(full)   // Signal filled slot
  END WHILE
END

CONSUMER:
BEGIN
  WHILE TRUE DO
    wait(full)     // Wait for filled slot
    wait(mutex)     // Enter critical section
    
    item := buffer[out]
    out := (out + 1) MOD BUFFER_SIZE
    
    signal(mutex)  // Exit critical section
    signal(empty)  // Signal empty slot
    
    consume_item(item)
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Three semaphores coordinate the producer-consumer problem. 'empty' counts available slots, 'full' counts filled slots, 'mutex' ensures only one process accesses the buffer at a time. This prevents both race conditions (using mutex) and synchronization issues (using empty/full).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for each semaphore operation, buffer operations are O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for buffer + O(n) for semaphore queues when processes blocked
